countries.name: United States
countries.notes: Employer-linked insurance means job loss compounds into healthcare loss. Weak safety net amplifies economic shocks.

countries.name: United Kingdom
countries.notes: NHS provides universal mental healthcare but wait times average 18 weeks. Housing crisis acute in London/SE.

countries.name: Germany
countries.notes: Strong social insurance system. Rental market protections buffer housing shocks.

countries.name: France
countries.notes: Generous unemployment benefits and universal healthcare substantially buffer economic shocks.

countries.name: Italy
countries.notes: Universal healthcare but low mental health service availability. Youth unemployment persistently high.

countries.name: Japan
countries.notes: Universal healthcare but mental health stigma limits access. Cultural factors moderate help-seeking.

countries.name: Australia
countries.notes: Medicare + Better Access scheme for mental health. Housing affordability crisis in Sydney/Melbourne.

countries.name: Canada
countries.notes: Universal healthcare but mental health services have long wait times. Severe housing affordability issues.

countries.name: Sweden
countries.notes: Strongest safety net in the dataset. Generous unemployment insurance and universal mental healthcare.

countries.name: Netherlands
countries.notes: Mandatory health insurance with mental health coverage. Rental market pressure increasing.

stressors.name: Housing Cost Burden

drivers.description: Median rent as a share of median household income
New housing starts vs. household formation rate

stressors.explanation: When housing costs exceed 30% of income, households face trade-offs between rent and healthcare, food, and social participation. The WHO identifies housing cost burden as a primary social determinant of mental health. Mortgage rates nearly doubled from the 2019-2020 baseline, locking out first-time buyers and increasing rental demand.
stressors.evidence_base: Bentley et al., 2011 (systematic review); Mason et al., 2013; WHO Social Determinants of Mental Health, 2014
stressors.mental_health_pathway: Housing cost >30% of income → financial anxiety → sleep disruption → reduced social participation → depression/anxiety risk (OR 1.3-1.5)
what_helps: Housing voucher programs reduced anxiety symptoms by 15% (Chetty, Hendren & Katz, 2016)
Rent stabilization policies reduce housing insecurity (Autor, Palmer & Pathak, 2014)
Financial counselling services reduce psychological distress from housing stress (Pleasence & Balmer, 2012)

stressors.name: Unemployment

drivers.description: Share of unemployed out of work >27 weeks
Includes involuntary part-time and discouraged workers

stressors.explanation: Unemployment is the single most studied economic determinant of mental health. The relationship is causal, not just correlational: longitudinal studies show mental health deteriorates after job loss and recovers after re-employment, controlling for prior health status. Long-term unemployment (>6 months) has compounding effects.
stressors.evidence_base: Paul & Moser, 2009 (meta-analysis, 237 studies); Clark & Oswald, 1994; Milner et al., 2013
stressors.mental_health_pathway: Job loss → income loss + identity disruption + social isolation → depression/anxiety (d=0.51). In countries without universal healthcare, job loss also means insurance loss, compounding the effect.
stressors.effect_size: d=0.51 for depression/anxiety (Paul & Moser, 2009 meta-analysis, N=237 studies)
what_helps: Active labour market programmes reduce depression risk by 10-15% (Puig-Barrachina et al., 2020)
Unemployment insurance generosity moderates the mental health impact (Norström & Grönqvist, 2015)
Job search support programmes reduce psychological distress alongside unemployment duration (Liu et al., 2014)

stressors.name: Inflation Pressure

drivers.description: Consumer price index for essential goods
University of Michigan or equivalent consumer confidence measure

stressors.explanation: Inflation erodes purchasing power, particularly for lower-income households who spend a larger share on food and energy. When wages don't keep pace, households face a cost-of-living squeeze that forces trade-offs between basic needs. Real wage growth turned negative in 2022 and has not fully recovered.
stressors.evidence_base: Rohde et al., 2016; Giorgi et al., 2020; Frasquilho et al., 2016 (systematic review)
stressors.mental_health_pathway: Inflation above wage growth → declining purchasing power → perceived financial insecurity → anxiety/stress → sleep disruption → depression (OR 1.1-1.3)
stressors.effect_size: OR 1.1-1.3 for anxiety/depression (Rohde et al., 2016); effect concentrated in lowest income quintiles
what_helps: Targeted cost-of-living payments reduce financial stress in lower-income households (Fetzer, 2019)
Free school meals and energy subsidies buffer essential spending pressure (Cooper & Stewart, 2013)
Financial literacy programmes reduce anxiety from economic uncertainty (Lusardi & Mitchell, 2014)

stressors.name: Consumer Debt

drivers.description: Total household debt as multiple of annual disposable income
Share of credit card balances 90+ days past due
Bankruptcy and individual voluntary arrangement filings per capita
drivers.source: US Courts / Insolvency Service (UK) / national statistics

stressors.explanation: Debt is a chronic stressor. Unlike unemployment (which can resolve with re-employment), debt compounds through interest and creates a persistent sense of being trapped. Credit card delinquency rates have risen significantly from the pre-pandemic baseline, indicating households are exhausting savings buffers built during the pandemic.
stressors.evidence_base: Sweet et al., 2013 (meta-analysis); Richardson et al., 2013; Fitch et al., 2011
stressors.mental_health_pathway: Rising debt → persistent financial worry → rumination → sleep disruption → depression/anxiety (OR 1.2-1.4). Debt-related shame compounds the effect through social withdrawal.
stressors.effect_size: OR 1.2-1.4 for depression; OR 1.9 for suicidal ideation among those with unmanageable debt (Richardson et al., 2013)
what_helps: Debt advice services reduce psychological distress by 50% within 12 months (Pleasence & Balmer, 2012)
Income-contingent repayment plans reduce anxiety compared to fixed repayment schedules (Chapman & Dearden, 2017)
Prohibition of aggressive debt collection practices reduces creditor-related anxiety (Drentea, 2000)

stressors.name: Income Inequality

drivers.description: Standard measure of income distribution inequality (0=equal, 1=unequal)
How far the median worker falls below the mean - measures top-skew of income
Share of national income held by the top decile

stressors.explanation: Income inequality is a slower-moving but structurally important stressor. It operates through relative deprivation (status anxiety), reduced social cohesion, and unequal access to protective resources (healthcare, housing, green space). Its effects are population-level rather than individual, making it harder to observe in short timeframes but significant over years.
stressors.evidence_base: Pickett & Wilkinson, 2015 (The Spirit Level); Layte, 2012; Patel et al., 2018 (Lancet Commission)
stressors.mental_health_pathway: Rising inequality → status anxiety + reduced social trust + unequal resource access → population-level increases in anxiety, depression, and substance use (r=0.47 across OECD nations)
stressors.effect_size: r=0.47 across OECD countries for inequality-mental illness correlation (Pickett & Wilkinson, 2015)
what_helps: Progressive taxation reduces inequality-driven status anxiety at the population level (Wilkinson & Pickett, 2018)
Universal basic services (healthcare, education, transport) buffer the effects of income disparity (Coote & Percy, 2020)
Community wealth-building approaches strengthen local economic resilience (CLES, 2019)
